title: User-defined fn — evaluation
analysis:
Currently `eval_expr(expr: &CompiledExpr, values: &ValueMap) -> Value` in reify-expr handles all CompiledExprKind variants except UserFunctionCall, which returns Value::Undef as a stub. Task 56 (dependency) added the compiler support: UserFunctionCall { function_name, args } nodes are emitted when a user function call is resolved, and CompiledFunction/CompiledFnBody store the function's param list, let bindings, and result expression in CompiledModule.functions.

The compiler's CompilationScope creates ValueCellId(fn_name, param_name) for function parameters, so function body expressions use ValueRef(ValueCellId('double', 'x')) to reference param 'x' of function 'double'. This convention must be matched at evaluation time when building the function scope.

Approach:
1. Move CompiledFunction and CompiledFnBody from reify-compiler to reify-types (they're pure data types using only reify-types primitives: String, Type, CompiledExpr, ContentHash). Re-export from reify-compiler for backward compat.
2. Create EvalContext struct in reify-expr with { values: &ValueMap, functions: &[CompiledFunction], recursion_depth: u32 }. Provides constructors: new(values, functions) and simple(values) (empty functions, depth 0).
3. Change eval_expr signature to take &EvalContext. Update all ~40 call sites across reify-eval (20+), reify-constraints (11), reify-runtime (1), and test files (4). Most callers use EvalContext::simple(values); Engine callers use EvalContext::new(values, &module.functions).
4. Implement UserFunctionCall evaluation: look up function by name+arity in ctx.functions, evaluate args, check Undef propagation, create fresh ValueMap scope with params bound, evaluate body let-bindings and result_expr.
5. Add recursion depth limit (1000) returning Undef on exceeded.
6. Wire functions through Engine evaluation paths.

The constraint subsystem (ConstraintInput, solver) also calls eval_expr. To support user functions in constraint expressions, add an optional functions field to ConstraintInput, or pass functions when constructing EvalContext in the solver. The practical approach: add `functions: &'a [CompiledFunction]` to ConstraintInput with a default of &[].

prerequisites.description: Move CompiledFunction and CompiledFnBody structs from crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs to crates/reify-types/src/expr.rs. These use only reify-types primitives (String, bool, Vec<(String,Type)>, Type, CompiledExpr, ContentHash). Add `pub use expr::{CompiledFunction, CompiledFnBody};` to reify-types/src/lib.rs. In reify-compiler/src/lib.rs, replace the struct definitions with `pub use reify_types::{CompiledFunction, CompiledFnBody};`. Verify: `cargo check -p reify-types -p reify-compiler -p reify-eval -p reify-constraints -p reify-runtime`.

Update CompiledModuleBuilder in reify-test-support/src/builders.rs to support functions. Add `functions: Vec<reify_types::CompiledFunction>` field (initialized to Vec::new()), a `.function(f: CompiledFunction) -> Self` builder method, and include functions in the build() output. Also include function content_hashes in the module's content_hash computation. This enables building test modules with user-defined functions for E2E tests.

Add `functions: &'a [CompiledFunction]` field to ConstraintInput in reify-types/src/constraint.rs. Default to &[] in all existing call sites that construct ConstraintInput (in reify-eval Engine check/build methods). This prepares the constraint subsystem to receive the function registry.

steps.description: Write failing test in reify-expr/src/lib.rs tests: `eval_user_fn_double`. Construct a CompiledFunction for `fn double(x: Real) -> Real { x + x }`: name='double', params=[('x', Type::Real)], return_type=Type::Real, body=CompiledFnBody { let_bindings: vec![], result_expr: BinOp(Add, ValueRef(ValueCellId('double','x')), ValueRef(ValueCellId('double','x'))) }. Build a UserFunctionCall expr: function_name='double', args=[Literal(Real(5.0))]. Currently this returns Undef. Write the test expecting Real(10.0) — it will fail, proving the feature is needed.

Create EvalContext struct and implement UserFunctionCall evaluation. This is the largest step:

1. In reify-expr/src/lib.rs, define:
   ```
   pub struct EvalContext<'a> {
       pub values: &'a ValueMap,
       pub functions: &'a [CompiledFunction],
       recursion_depth: u32,
   }
   ```
   With constructors: `new(values, functions)` (depth=0), `simple(values)` (functions=&[], depth=0).
   Private helper: `with_scope(values)` returns new EvalContext with same functions, depth+1.

2. Change `eval_expr` signature to `eval_expr(expr: &CompiledExpr, ctx: &EvalContext) -> Value`.

3. Thread ctx through all internal functions: eval_binop, eval_unop, eval_and, eval_or now take `ctx: &EvalContext` instead of `values: &ValueMap`, reading values from `ctx.values`.

4. Implement UserFunctionCall arm:
   - Find function by name in ctx.functions (first match)
   - Evaluate arg expressions against ctx
   - If any arg is Undef, return Undef (strict propagation)
   - Check recursion_depth < MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH (1000), return Undef if exceeded
   - Create fresh ValueMap, bind params: ValueCellId(fn_name, param_name) -> arg_value
   - Evaluate let_bindings in order, inserting into scope
   - Evaluate result_expr with new scope

5. Update ALL callers:
   - reify-eval/src/lib.rs: ~20 sites → EvalContext::simple(&values) initially (Engine integration in step-16)
   - reify-constraints/src/lib.rs: 1 site → EvalContext::simple(input.values)
   - reify-constraints/src/solver.rs: ~10 sites → EvalContext::simple(values)
   - reify-runtime/src/concurrent_eval.rs: 1 site → EvalContext::simple(&current_values)
   - reify-eval/tests/boundary2_consumer.rs: 1 site → EvalContext::simple(&values)
   - reify-compiler/tests/boundary2_producer.rs: 3 sites → EvalContext::simple(&values)
   - Internal tests in reify-expr: all sites → EvalContext::simple(&values)

6. Verify all existing tests pass (only API change, no behavior change for non-UserFunctionCall exprs).

Write test: `eval_user_fn_with_let_bindings`. Construct `fn f(x: Real) -> Real { let y = x + 1; y * 2 }` — CompiledFunction with body.let_bindings = [('y', BinOp(Add, ValueRef('f','x'), Literal(Int(1))))] and body.result_expr = BinOp(Mul, ValueRef('f','y'), Literal(Int(2))). Create EvalContext with functions=[f]. Call f(4) via UserFunctionCall, expect Real(10.0). Tests that let bindings in function bodies evaluate correctly and are available to the result expression.

Fix let binding evaluation in function body if step-3 fails. Ensure each let binding's expression is evaluated against the current scope (which grows as bindings are added), and the binding result is inserted as ValueCellId(fn_name, binding_name). The step-2 implementation should already handle this — if the test passes, this is a no-op confirmation step.

Write test: `eval_user_fn_recursive_factorial`. Construct `fn factorial(n: Int) -> Int { if n <= 1 then 1 else n * factorial(n - 1) }`. Body result_expr is Conditional { condition: BinOp(Le, ValueRef('factorial','n'), Literal(Int(1))), then_branch: Literal(Int(1)), else_branch: BinOp(Mul, ValueRef('factorial','n'), UserFunctionCall('factorial', [BinOp(Sub, ValueRef('factorial','n'), Literal(Int(1)))])) }. Call factorial(5), expect Int(120). Tests that recursion works: function re-enters eval with a new scope frame preserving the function registry.

Ensure recursion works. The UserFunctionCall evaluator uses ctx.with_scope(&new_values) which preserves the functions slice and increments depth. The recursive UserFunctionCall in the body re-enters the same code path with new param bindings. If factorial test passes, this is a no-op confirmation. If not, debug scope construction or depth threading.

Write test: `eval_user_fn_recursion_depth_exceeded`. Construct `fn infinite(x: Int) -> Int { infinite(x) }` — unconditionally recursive. Call infinite(1), assert result is Value::Undef (should not stack-overflow, should hit the 1000-depth limit and return Undef gracefully).

Ensure depth limit works. The step-2 implementation checks `ctx.recursion_depth >= MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH` at the top of UserFunctionCall handling and returns Undef. If the test passes, no-op. If it stack-overflows, the depth check is missing or not incrementing — fix accordingly.

Write test: `eval_user_fn_undef_arg_propagation`. Call double(Undef) where double is the fn from step-1. Assert result is Value::Undef. Tests strict Undef propagation: if any argument evaluates to Undef, the function call short-circuits without evaluating the body.
Ensure Undef arg propagation works. Step-2 implementation checks `if evaluated_args.iter().any(|v| v.is_undef()) { return Value::Undef; }` before entering the function body. If test passes, no-op.

Write test: `eval_user_fn_dimension_args`. Construct `fn area(w: Length, h: Length) -> Area { w * h }` with Scalar-typed params. Call area(80mm, 100mm) with args Scalar{si_value:0.08, dim:LENGTH}, Scalar{si_value:0.1, dim:LENGTH}. Assert result is Scalar{si_value:0.008, dim:AREA}. Tests that dimensional Scalar values flow correctly through function evaluation.

Dimension handling should work automatically since the function body's BinOp(Mul) is evaluated by the existing eval_mul which combines dimension vectors. If the test passes, no-op. If not, debug the scope binding for Scalar values.

Write test: `eval_user_fn_overload_by_arity`. Create two functions: `fn process(x: Real) -> Real { x * 2 }` and `fn process(x: Real, y: Real) -> Real { x + y }`. Call process(3.0) with 1 arg → expect Real(6.0). Call process(3.0, 4.0) with 2 args → expect Real(7.0). Tests that runtime lookup matches by name AND argument count.

Implement arity-based overload resolution. In the UserFunctionCall handler, when looking up by name, filter candidates by `fn.params.len() == args.len()`. If exactly one matches, use it. If zero or multiple match, return Undef. Update the lookup from simple name match to name+arity match.

Write E2E integration test in new file crates/reify-eval/tests/fn_eval.rs. Parse and compile source text: `fn double(x: Real) -> Real { x + x }\nstructure S { let v = double(3) }`. Use reify_syntax::parse() and reify_compiler::compile(). Create Engine with mock constraint checker, call engine.eval(&module). Assert the value for ValueCellId('S','v') == Real(6.0). This validates the complete pipeline from source text through compilation to evaluation, including the engine threading functions to eval_expr.

Wire functions through Engine evaluation. Update reify-eval/src/lib.rs:
1. In Engine::eval(), change all eval_expr calls from EvalContext::simple(&values) to EvalContext::new(&values, &module.functions).
2. In Engine::eval_cached(), same change — module is available as parameter.
3. In evaluate_let_bindings(), add a `functions: &[CompiledFunction]` parameter, pass to EvalContext::new.
4. In Engine::check() and Engine::build(), pass module.functions to constraint input and eval contexts.
5. In the concurrent eval path (reify-runtime/src/concurrent_eval.rs), add a functions field to ConcurrentEvalAdapter (populated from the module). Use it when constructing EvalContext.
6. In reify-constraints callers, use input.functions from ConstraintInput.
7. Run full test suite to verify everything works end-to-end.

design_decisions.decision: Move CompiledFunction and CompiledFnBody from reify-compiler to reify-types
design_decisions.rationale: These are pure data types composed entirely of reify-types primitives (String, bool, Type, CompiledExpr, ContentHash). Moving them follows the existing pattern where CompiledExpr, CompiledExprKind, CompiledMatchArm already live in reify-types. This avoids adding reify-compiler as a dependency of reify-expr, which would be architecturally backwards. Re-exporting from reify-compiler maintains backward compatibility.

design_decisions.decision: Change eval_expr signature to take &EvalContext (breaking change), update all callers
design_decisions.rationale: The task explicitly requires this approach (Option 2). While a backward-compatible wrapper would minimize churn, the breaking change ensures all evaluation paths can support user functions uniformly. The migration is mechanical: most callers change `reify_expr::eval_expr(expr, &values)` to `reify_expr::eval_expr(expr, &EvalContext::simple(&values))`. This is a one-time cost that results in a cleaner API.

design_decisions.decision: EvalContext recursion_depth field is private with no public setter
design_decisions.rationale: Callers should never set recursion depth — it's internal bookkeeping for preventing stack overflow. Only the UserFunctionCall evaluator increments it via the private with_scope() helper. This prevents misuse while keeping the public API clean.

design_decisions.decision: Function scope is fully isolated (fresh ValueMap, no access to caller's values)
design_decisions.rationale: User-defined functions in Reify are pure. The compiler's CompilationScope creates ValueCellId(fn_name, param_name) for parameters, meaning function body expressions only reference these IDs. A fresh ValueMap with only param and let-binding entries naturally enforces this isolation. The function registry (ctx.functions) is carried through for nested calls.

design_decisions.decision: Runtime overload resolution by name + argument count
design_decisions.rationale: The compiler already performs full type-based overload resolution at compile time. At runtime, name + arity is sufficient to distinguish overloads. This avoids threading type information through the evaluator and keeps the lookup simple.

design_decisions.decision: Recursion depth limit of 1000 returning Value::Undef
design_decisions.rationale: 1000 allows deep legitimate recursion while preventing stack overflow. Returning Undef follows Reify's philosophy where failed/impossible computations produce Undef, which propagates upward cleanly. A diagnostic message would be ideal but Undef is sufficient for v0.1.

design_decisions.decision: Add functions field to ConstraintInput struct
design_decisions.rationale: Constraint expressions can contain UserFunctionCall nodes. The solver evaluates these expressions repeatedly during optimization. Adding functions to ConstraintInput is the least-invasive way to provide the function registry to the constraint subsystem. Defaults to &[] for backward compatibility.

reuse.what: Existing eval infrastructure (eval_binop, eval_unop, eval_add, eval_mul, Undef propagation, dimensional arithmetic)
reuse.how: Function body expressions are standard CompiledExpr trees. Once the scope ValueMap is set up with param bindings, the entire evaluation infrastructure handles body evaluation without modification. Only the top-level threading changes from ValueMap to EvalContext.

The compiler creates ValueCellId(fn_name, param_name) for function parameters. The evaluator mirrors this convention when binding arguments: ValueCellId('double', 'x') → arg_value. This ensures ValueRef nodes in the compiled body resolve correctly.

reuse.what: ValueMap with PersistentMap (im::HashMap) for O(1) structural-sharing clones
reuse.how: Each function call creates a new fresh ValueMap for its scope. The pattern of creating isolated child ValueMaps already exists in the engine's sub-component evaluation (child_values). PersistentMap makes this cheap.

reuse.what: Test expression builders (literal, value_ref_typed, binop, etc.)
reuse.how: Use these to construct function body expressions and call argument expressions in unit tests, avoiding manual CompiledExpr construction.

reuse.what: E2E test pattern: parse → compile → Engine.eval() → assert values
reuse.how: Follow the established E2E testing pattern for the integration test. Use reify_syntax::parse(), reify_compiler::compile(), Engine::new(), engine.eval(&module), then check result values.
After adding .function() support, use the builder to construct modules with both functions and structures for programmatic E2E tests.
